---
title: "Study Design"
subtitle: "Motivation, data, evaluation protocol, and search procedure"
---

## Research question

Can an LLM-guided search procedure improve pseudo-real-time macroeconomic forecasts by selecting data representations, covariates, and fine-tuning settings for a time series foundation model? And do the improvements generalize out of sample?

We test this across **three small open economies** --- Norway, Canada, and Sweden --- running an automated search loop over the pipeline configuration of **Chronos-2** (120M parameters) and comparing against classical and multivariate baselines under a strict rolling pseudo-real-time evaluation protocol.

## Countries

| Country | Target variables | Data sources |
|---------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Norway** | CPI, industrial production, retail sales, unemployment | SSB, Norges Bank, FRED |
| **Canada** | CPI, industrial production, retail sales, unemployment | Statistics Canada, Bank of Canada, FRED |
| **Sweden** | CPI, industrial production, unemployment | SCB, Riksbank, FRED |

Sweden has 3 targets (retail sales excluded due to insufficient history).


## Evaluation protocol

### Rolling expanding-window design

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-protocol
//| fig-cap: "Evaluation timeline. Each row represents a forecast origin. The model uses all available data up to the origin (blue) and produces forecasts at h=1,3,6,12 months ahead (orange dots)."

Plot = import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm")

{
  const origins = [
    {origin: new Date("2006-06-30"), label: "2006-06"},
    {origin: new Date("2009-01-31"), label: "2009-01"},
    {origin: new Date("2012-06-30"), label: "2012-06"},
    {origin: new Date("2015-12-31"), label: "2015-12"},
    {origin: new Date("2020-01-31"), label: "2020-01"},
  ];

  const horizons = [1, 3, 6, 12];
  const data = [];

  for (const o of origins) {
    data.push({
      origin: o.label,
      x1: new Date("1990-01-01"),
      x2: o.origin,
      type: "Training data"
    });
    for (const h of horizons) {
      const target = new Date(o.origin);
      target.setMonth(target.getMonth() + h);
      data.push({
        origin: o.label,
        x: target,
        h: h,
        type: "Forecast"
      });
    }
  }

  const bars = data.filter(d => d.type === "Training data");
  const dots = data.filter(d => d.type === "Forecast");

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 220,
    marginLeft: 80,
    marginBottom: 40,
    x: {type: "utc", label: "Date", domain: [new Date("1988-01-01"), new Date("2022-01-01")]},
    y: {label: "Origin", domain: origins.map(o => o.label).reverse()},
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleX([new Date("2016-01-01")], {stroke: "#e74c3c", strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
      Plot.text([{x: new Date("2016-01-01"), text: "Test era ->"}], {
        x: "x", text: "text", dy: -8, dx: 5, fontSize: 9, fill: "#e74c3c", textAnchor: "start"
      }),
      Plot.barX(bars, {
        x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y: "origin",
        fill: "#3498db", fillOpacity: 0.4
      }),
      Plot.dot(origins.map(o => ({x: o.origin, y: o.label})), {
        x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#2c3e50", r: 5
      }),
      Plot.dot(dots, {
        x: "x", y: "origin",
        fill: d => ({"1": "#f39c12", "3": "#e67e22", "6": "#d35400", "12": "#c0392b"})[d.h],
        r: 4,
        tip: true, title: d => `h=${d.h}`
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

| Era | Period | Origins | Purpose |
|-----|--------|---------|---------|
| Validation | 2006-01 to 2015-12 | 120 monthly | Search, model selection |
| Test | 2016-01 to 2025-03 | 111 monthly | Final out-of-sample (frozen) |

Test-era subperiods: pre-COVID (2016--19), COVID (2020--21), post-COVID/inflation (2022+).


## Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| **RMSE** | sqrt(mean((y - y_hat)^2)) | Penalizes large errors; in original units |
| **MAE** | mean(\|y - y_hat\|) | Average absolute error; in original units |
| **MASE** | MAE / MAE_naive | Scale-free; < 1 beats random walk |

**MASE** is the primary search metric because it is comparable across variables with different scales.


## Baselines

| Method | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Random walk | Naive | Forecast = last observed value |
| Seasonal naive | Naive | Forecast = same month last year |
| AR(p) | Univariate | Direct OLS, lag order by BIC |
| ARIMA(p,d,q) | Univariate | AIC grid search |
| ETS | Univariate | Auto-selected trend/seasonal via AIC |
| VAR | Multivariate | Vector autoregression |
| BVAR | Multivariate | Bayesian VAR with Minnesota shrinkage |
| Factor model | Multivariate | Principal components regression |
| Elastic Net | ML | L1+L2 regularized regression |


## Foundation model: Chronos-2

**amazon/chronos-2** (120M parameters) --- a universal time series transformer pretrained on billions of observations. Unlike earlier Chronos-Bolt models, Chronos-2 natively integrates covariate information during inference.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Parameters | 120M |
| Architecture | Transformer (universal) |
| Context length | Up to 8,192 time steps |
| Covariate support | Past covariates + known future covariates |
| Fine-tuning | LoRA (r=8, alpha=16) via AutoGluon |
| Interface | AutoGluon `"Chronos-2"` model key |


## Search procedure

An LLM (**Claude Sonnet**) proposes pipeline configurations based on domain knowledge and past results. Each iteration:

1. Claude reads: task description, search space, current best config, iteration history
2. Claude proposes a JSON config (covariates, transforms, context length, fine-tuning)
3. **Quick evaluation** on 20 subsampled origins (~30s)
4. If improved: **full evaluation** on all 120 origins (~3 min)
5. Accept if full score improves; reject otherwise
6. Log iteration, update state, continue

### Search variants tested

| Variant | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Informed LLM** | Claude with country-specific domain knowledge |
| **Blind LLM** | Claude with generic instructions, no country context |
| **Random** | Uniform random sampling from search space |
| **Greedy** | One-at-a-time covariate addition |
| **Manual economist** | Hand-selected config based on domain expertise |


### Search space

| Dimension | Range | Default |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| Covariates | Any subset of available variables | None |
| Covariate transforms | none, log_diff, pct_change_1/12, standardize, MA | None |
| Context length | 24, 36, 48, 64, 96, 128, unlimited | Unlimited |
| Fine-tuning | Off / LoRA | Off |
| Fine-tune steps | 100, 500, 1000, 2000 | 1000 |
| Fine-tune LR | 1e-6 to 1e-4 (log scale) | 1e-5 |
| Grouping | Univariate / All targets | Univariate |


## Reproducibility

| Component | Version / Setting |
|-----------|------------------|
| Python | 3.11 |
| chronos-forecasting | 2.2.2 |
| autogluon.timeseries | 1.5.0 |
| PyTorch | 2.9.1 (CUDA 12.8) |
| Hardware | NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB) |
| Origin subsample seed | 42 |
| Dependency lock | `uv.lock` |
